symptoms.symptom_name: Acute large-volume watery diarrhoea following suspected gluten exposure

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Large-volume watery diarrhoea shortly after gluten exposure in a known coeliac is the hallmark of a flare.
Acute watery diarrhoea is non-specific and can be infective; discriminated by fever/contacts/travel.

symptoms.symptom_name: Diffuse periumbilical crampy (colicky) abdominal pain, non-localised, no migration

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Diffuse crampy colic accompanies the hyperactive gut of an enteropathic flare.
Diffuse non-localised periumbilical pain without migration to the right lower quadrant argues against appendicitis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Orthostatic presyncope (near-faint on standing), no actual syncope

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Hypovolaemia secondary to gastrointestinal fluid loss
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Postural lightheadedness is a sensitive marker of volume depletion from fluid loss.

symptoms.symptom_name: Severe nausea preventing oral fluid intake

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Hypovolaemia secondary to gastrointestinal fluid loss
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Inability to replace ongoing losses accelerates volume depletion and defines the rehydration/antiemetic management target.
Nausea commonly accompanies an acute enteropathic flare.

symptoms.symptom_name: Known coeliac disease with multiple identical prior gluten-triggered flares

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: An established diagnosis with a stereotyped prior flare pattern after gluten strongly raises the prior probability of the same benign flare.
A recognised recurrent benign pattern lowers (but does not eliminate) the probability of a new surgical cause.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of fever, sick contacts and recent travel

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: No fever, contacts or travel and immediate onset after a known trigger make an infective cause less likely.

symptoms.symptom_name: Soft, non-distended abdomen with no guarding, rebound or localised tenderness

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of peritonism, guarding or localised tenderness on examination strongly argues against an acute surgical abdomen — sealed clinician finding the AI Doctor cannot obtain remotely and must instead screen for symptomatically and safety-net.
Pain proportionate to a soft benign abdomen argues against the classic 'pain out of proportion' of mesenteric ischaemia.

symptoms.symptom_name: Tachycardia (HR 108), low-normal BP (100/65), delayed capillary refill, dry mucous membranes, sluggish skin turgor

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Hypovolaemia secondary to gastrointestinal fluid loss
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Objective compensated volume depletion — sealed clinician measurement; the AI Doctor infers dehydration from reported presyncope, reduced urine output and inability to keep fluids down, not from a measured BP/HR.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of blood in stool or melaena

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absent haematochezia/melaena lowers the probability of ischaemic bowel and GI bleeding; a positive result would reinstate them as emergencies.
A benign coeliac flare produces watery, non-bloody diarrhoea.

symptoms.symptom_name: Serum electrolytes, renal function and micronutrient panel (would be checked at GP follow-up)

investigation_details.investigation_name: Serum electrolytes, urea, creatinine and coeliac nutritional panel (iron, folate, B12, vitamin D, magnesium, zinc)
investigation_details.expected_result: May show mild dehydration-related biochemistry and chronic malabsorption micronutrient deficiencies — arranged at GP follow-up, not obtainable acutely via telehealth.
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Electrolyte disturbance and nutritional deficiencies from ongoing malabsorption go unmonitored.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Hypovolaemia secondary to gastrointestinal fluid loss
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Confirms and quantifies volume depletion and micronutrient status; a monitoring investigation rather than a diagnostic one.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Watery diarrhoea after gluten + diffuse non-localised crampy pain + known coeliac with identical prior flares + no fever/contacts/travel + no blood in stool constitutes the reassuring benign-flare constellation that permits home management with safety-netting rather than emergency escalation.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Hypovolaemia secondary to gastrointestinal fluid loss
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Orthostatic presyncope + inability to keep fluids down + reduced urine output (with clinician-measured tachycardia) define compensated mild hypovolaemia — the actionable acute risk driving oral rehydration and the dehydration escalation triggers.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Serum electrolytes, urea and creatinine + coeliac nutritional panel
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Arrange at GP follow-up in 3-5 days to check hydration biochemistry and screen for malabsorption micronutrient deficiencies — not required acutely and not obtainable via telehealth.

Not indicated for a stable, non-surgical, non-bleeding coeliac flare with a soft benign abdomen; ordering it reflects over-investigation of a benign presentation. It becomes relevant only if surgical red flags develop, at which point in-person ED assessment (not a telehealth order) is the pathway.
